questions.title: Typedef for Struct
options: Creates a new type incompatible with struct
questions.explanation: typedef creates an alias. You can use 'Point' instead of 'struct Point'.

questions.title: Complete find_last Function
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens into the blanks to complete the find_last function that finds the last node with a matching key:
questions.explanation: We initialize r to NULL to track the last matching node. We traverse the list while node is not NULL, check if the current node's key matches k, and update r when we find a match. Finally, we return r which points to the last matching node (or NULL if none found).
hints: Initialize r to NULL since we haven't found any matches yet
Use != to check if node is not NULL
Use -> to access struct members through a pointer
Update r to point to the current node when keys match

questions.title: Struct Pointer Access
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to access a struct member:
questions.explanation: Use -> to access members through a pointer.

questions.title: Complete Struct Initialization
questions.description: Drag tokens to initialize and access a struct:
questions.explanation: Struct pointer: parameter is pointer (*p), use arrow operator (->) to access members through pointer, += to modify values.

questions.title: Complete Linked List Insert at Head
questions.description: Drag tokens to insert a node at the head of a linked list:
questions.explanation: Insert at head: pointer to pointer (node_t**) to modify head, allocate new node, set data, point new_node->next to current head, update head to new_node.
hints: Double pointer (**) needed to modify head
New node points to old head

questions.title: Complete Typedef Struct Definition (Exam Style)
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete a typedef struct definition for a point with coordinates:
questions.code_template: [___] [___] point { [___] x; [___] y; } [___];
questions.explanation: Typedef struct pattern: typedef struct name { fields } alias; Creates alias point_t for struct point.
hints: typedef creates an alias for a type
Fields should match the data type needed

questions.title: Complete Linked List Node Typedef (Exam Style)
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete a typedef for a linked list node:
questions.code_template: [___] [___] node { int data; [___] node [___]next; } [___];
questions.explanation: Self-referential struct: must use 'struct node' for the next pointer since node_t isn't defined yet inside the struct body.

questions.title: Complete Person Struct (Exam Style)
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete a typedef struct for a person with name and age:
questions.code_template: [___] [___] { [___] name[50]; [___] age; } [___];
questions.explanation: typedef struct pattern with array member: char name[50] for string, int for age, person_t as alias.

questions.title: Complete Rectangle Struct with Pointer (Exam Style)
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete a typedef struct for a rectangle with pointer to center point:
questions.explanation: Nested struct with pointer: point_t* for pointer to center, typedef struct pattern creates rect_t alias.

questions.title: Complete Array of Structs Initialization (Exam Style)
questions.description: Drag tokens to initialize an array of point structs:
questions.explanation: Array of structs initialization: array size [2], outer braces {}, each struct element in inner braces {{x, y}, {x, y}}.

questions.title: Dot vs Arrow
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about struct access:

questions.text: Use the ___ operator to access members via a pointer
Use the ___ operator to access members of a value

questions.explanation: Use -> for pointers and . for struct values.

questions.title: Struktur Terminologi (Danish)

questions.text: ___ operatoren får adgang til struct medlemmer gennem en variabel, ___ gennem en pointer
Variabler inde i en struct kaldes ___
Ekstra bytes tilføjet for alignment kaldes ___

questions.explanation: Struct terminologi: medlemmer/felter (variabler indeni), punktum operator (direkte adgang), pil operator (pointer adgang), padding (alignment bytes).

questions.title: Structure Terminology
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about structures:

questions.text: The ___ operator accesses struct members through a variable, ___ through a pointer
Variables inside a struct are called ___
Extra bytes added for alignment are called ___

questions.explanation: Struct terminology: members/fields (variables inside), dot operator (direct access), arrow operator (pointer access), padding (alignment bytes).

questions.title: Implementer linked list insertion (Exam Style)
hints: Håndter tre tilfælde: tom liste, indsæt først, indsæt i midten/slutningen
Brug malloc(sizeof(node_t)) til at allokere ny node
Opdater head pointer gennem **head hvis nødvendigt
restrictions: MUST use malloc to allocate nodes

questions.title: Implementer linked list remove duplicates (Exam Style)
hints: Loop gennem listen og sammenlign current->data med current->next->data
Husk at kalde free() på den fjernede node
Opdater current->next til at springe over duplikaten

questions.title: Implementer linked list find nth from end (Exam Style)
hints: Brug two-pointer teknik: flyt en pointer n steps frem først
Derefter flyt begge pointere samtidig indtil den forreste når slutningen
Den bageste pointer vil nu være ved n'te node fra slutningen
Håndter edge case hvor n > list length

questions.title: Implementer linked list reverse (Exam Style)
hints: Brug tre pointere: prev, current, next
Loop gennem listen og vend hver node's next pointer
Opdater head til at pege på den tidligere sidste node
Håndter edge case: tom liste eller liste med én node

questions.title: Simple Tree Node Count
questions.explanation: The tree has 3 nodes: root (n1) with two children (n2 and n3). count_nodes returns 1 + count_nodes(left) + count_nodes(right) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3.
hints: Count all nodes in the tree

questions.title: Inorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits: left subtree, root, right subtree. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so inorder prints: 1, 2, 3.

questions.title: Preorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Preorder traversal visits: root, left subtree, right subtree. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so preorder prints: 2, 1, 3.

questions.title: Postorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Postorder traversal visits: left subtree, right subtree, root. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so postorder prints: 1, 3, 2.

questions.title: Tree Height Calculation
questions.explanation: The tree is a chain: 1->2->3. Height counts nodes from root to deepest leaf: 3 nodes = height 3.
hints: Count nodes from root to leaf

questions.title: Tree Sum Recursive
questions.explanation: Sum of all node values: 2 + 3 + 5 = 10.

questions.title: Calculate Struct Size
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Example)? Assume: char=1, int=4, double=8, pointer=8 bytes
questions.explanation: With padding: char a (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding + int b (4 bytes) + char c (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding = 12 bytes total. The struct is padded to align to its largest member (int, 4 bytes).
hints: Padding is added after 'a' and after 'c'

questions.title: Arrow vs Dot
questions.explanation: ptr points to p, so ptr->y accesses p.y, which is 2.

questions.title: Struct Assignment Copy
questions.explanation: Struct assignment copies values. b is a copy of a, so changing b.x does not change a.x.
hints: b is independent after the copy

questions.title: Struct Initialization
questions.explanation: p.b is initialized to 4.
hints: Initializer list sets a then b

questions.title: Padding with Double
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Example)? Assume: char=1, double=8, alignment to 8 bytes.
questions.explanation: The double must be 8-byte aligned. The struct becomes 16 bytes due to padding after the char.

questions.title: Pointer Member Access
questions.explanation: p->id accesses it.id, which is 42.

questions.title: Pass by Value
questions.explanation: The struct is passed by value, so set_x modifies a copy, not the original.

questions.title: sizeof Struct vs Pointer
questions.description: What does this program print on a 64-bit system? Assume int=4 bytes.
questions.explanation: Two ints make 8 bytes. The pointer size is also 8 on 64-bit.

questions.title: Nested Struct
questions.explanation: o.in.v is initialized to 5.

questions.title: Array of Structs
questions.explanation: arr[1].x accesses the second struct, which has x = 2.

questions.title: typedef Struct
questions.explanation: typedef creates an alias. it.id is 9.

questions.title: Modify via Pointer
questions.explanation: Passing a pointer allows modifying the original struct. a.x becomes 7.

questions.title: Padding with Mixed Types
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Mix)? Assume: char=1, int=4, double=8, alignment to 8 bytes.
questions.explanation: Layout: char (1) + 3 padding + int (4) = 8 so far, then double (8) = 16. The struct aligns to 8, so total size is 16.

questions.title: Struct Copy in Function
questions.explanation: The function returns a struct by value. a.x is initialized to 5.
hints: Returning a struct by value is allowed

questions.title: Self-Referential Struct
questions.description: Which line correctly declares a self-referential pointer?
questions.explanation: A struct can contain a pointer to its own type: struct Node *next;

questions.title: Struct Member Access
Struct Padding
questions.description: What is likely the sizeof this struct on a typical system?
questions.explanation: Due to alignment requirements, padding is added. char (1) + padding (3) + int (4) + char (1) + padding (3) = 12 bytes.
hints: Total is usually a multiple of largest type

questions.title: Struct Assignment
questions.explanation: Struct assignment creates a copy. p2 is a separate copy of p1. Changing p2.x doesn't affect p1.x.

questions.title: Nested Structs
questions.explanation: Nested struct initialization: {{42}} initializes o.in.val to 42. Access with o.in.val.

questions.title: Struct Pointers
questions.description: What's the correct way to access member 'x' through pointer 'p'?
questions.explanation: Both (*p).x and p->x access members through pointer. p->x is shorthand for (*p).x.

questions.title: Array of Structs Initialization
questions.explanation: points[1] is the second struct with x=3, y=4. points[1].y is 4.

questions.title: Struct Pointer Array
questions.explanation: arr[1] is a pointer to d2. arr[1]->val accesses d2.val which is 20.

questions.title: Struct Designated Initializers
questions.explanation: Designated initializers set specific fields. Uninitialized fields default to 0. x=1, y=0, z=3.

questions.title: Struct Literal
questions.explanation: The compound literal initializes a=10 and b=20.

questions.title: Pointer Member Access
questions.explanation: (*p).x modifies s.x through the pointer, setting it to 9.

questions.title: Array of Structs Size
questions.description: What is sizeof(arr) if struct S has size 8?
questions.code_template:
struct S { int a; int b; };
struct S arr[3];
questions.explanation: Three elements each 8 bytes gives 24 bytes.

questions.title: Struct Assignment
questions.explanation: Struct assignment copies values. b.x remains 1 after a changes.

questions.title: Nested Access
questions.explanation: Nested struct is initialized with v = 7.

questions.title: Padding Question
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct T)? Assume char=1, int=4, double=8.
questions.explanation: double (8) + char (1) + padding (3) + int (4) = 16. Alignment to 8 keeps it at 16.

questions.title: Pointer Alias
questions.explanation: p and q point to the same struct. Updating via q updates s.x.

questions.title: Struct as Function Param
questions.explanation: The struct is passed by value, so a.x is unchanged.

questions.title: Struct Pointer Param
questions.explanation: Passing a pointer allows modifying the original struct.

questions.title: sizeof with Padding
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct S)? Assume char=1, int=4, alignment to 4.
questions.explanation: Two chars (2) + padding (2) + int (4) = 8.

questions.title: Self-referential Pointer
questions.description: Which is valid inside a struct definition?
questions.explanation: A struct can contain a pointer to its own type.

questions.title: Struct Array Access
questions.explanation: a[1].x accesses the second element's x, which is 3.

questions.title: Bit-field Size
questions.description: What is the size of this struct? Assume unsigned int is 4 bytes.
questions.explanation: Bit-fields are packed into an unsigned int, so size is 4 bytes.

questions.title: Pointer to Struct
questions.explanation: (*p).x accesses s.x, which is 7.

questions.title: Copy Struct Return
questions.explanation: Structs can be returned by value. a.x is 3.

questions.title: Struct Comparison
options: a == b is allowed in C
questions.explanation: C does not allow == comparison for structs.

questions.title: Struct Zero Init
questions.explanation: Initializing with {0} sets all fields to 0.

questions.title: Flexible Array Member
questions.explanation: Flexible array members (C99) allow variable-length arrays at the end of structs. The allocation reserves space for the struct plus 5 extra bytes for data[].
hints: Flexible array member is a C99 feature
Must be last member in struct
Memory allocated for size + extra bytes

questions.title: Bit Fields Basic
questions.explanation: Bit fields allow packing multiple values into a single storage unit. flag1 and flag2 each use 1 bit, value uses 6 bits.
hints: Values fit within specified bit widths

questions.title: Bit Field Overflow
questions.explanation: A 3-bit field can hold values 0-7. The value 10 (binary 1010) is truncated to 010 (2) when stored in 3 bits.

questions.title: Anonymous Union
questions.explanation: Anonymous unions (C11) allow direct access to union members without naming the union. d.i accesses the int member directly.

questions.title: Anonymous Struct in Union
questions.explanation: The result depends on endianness. On little-endian systems it would be 0x3412, on big-endian 0x1234. The anonymous struct shares memory with the short.

questions.title: Pragma Pack Effect
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Packed) with #pragma pack(1)? Assume char=1, int=4 without packing.
questions.explanation: #pragma pack(1) removes all padding. The struct is exactly char(1) + int(4) + char(1) = 6 bytes.

questions.title: Pragma Pack Comparison
questions.description: How do sizes compare? Assume char=1, int=4, normal alignment=4.
questions.explanation: Normal struct has padding: char(1) + pad(3) + int(4) = 8. Packed struct has no padding: char(1) + int(4) = 5.
hints: Normal struct adds padding for alignment

questions.title: Linked List Node
questions.explanation: n2.next points to n1, so n2.next->data accesses n1.data which is 10.

questions.title: Linked List Traversal
questions.explanation: p points to n1, p->next is n2, p->next->next is n3, so p->next->next->val is 3.

questions.title: Compound Literal Initialization
questions.explanation: Compound literal with designated initializers. Order doesn't matter with designated initializers: x=3, y=5.
hints: Designated initializers can be in any order

questions.title: Partial Designated Initialization
questions.explanation: With designated initializers, unspecified members are zero-initialized. Only b is set to 10, a and c are 0.

questions.title: Memory Layout with Reordering
questions.description: Which struct layout is most memory efficient? Assume char=1, int=4, double=8, alignment to 8.
questions.explanation: A: char(1)+pad(7)+double(8)+int(4)+char(1)+pad(3)=24. B: double(8)+int(4)+char(1)+char(1)+pad(2)=16. Placing larger members first reduces padding.

questions.title: Struct Comparison with memcmp
questions.description: What does this program likely print?
questions.explanation: memcmp compares bytes including padding. Padding bytes have undefined values, so comparison may fail even when member values match.

questions.title: Struct Equality Comparison
options: p1 == p2 compiles and returns true
p1 == p2 compiles and returns false
questions.explanation: C does not allow direct comparison of structs with ==. You must compare members individually or use memcmp (with care).
hints: No built-in struct equality in C

questions.title: Pass Large Struct by Value
questions.description: What is the main concern with this code?
questions.explanation: Passing large structs by value creates a full copy (10KB here), which is inefficient. Pass by pointer instead for better performance.

questions.title: Pass by Pointer for Efficiency
questions.explanation: sizeof(d) gives the size of the pointer (8 on 64-bit), not the struct. sizeof(*d) would give the struct size.

questions.title: Flexible Array Member Size
questions.explanation: Flexible array members don't contribute to the struct size. sizeof returns only the size of the fixed members (just length here).
hints: Flexible array has zero size in sizeof

questions.title: Anonymous Struct Members
questions.explanation: Anonymous struct allows direct access to members. o.z directly accesses the z field which is 3.

questions.title: Bit Field Signed vs Unsigned
questions.explanation: A signed 2-bit field can hold -2 to 1. Value 3 (binary 11) is interpreted as -1 in two's complement.

questions.title: Bit Field Alignment Padding
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Mixed)? Assume int=4 bytes.
questions.explanation: The two bit fields (16 bits total) are packed into one int (4 bytes), followed by another int c (4 bytes) = 8 bytes total.
hints: a and b share first 4 bytes

questions.title: Struct Array Initialization
questions.explanation: The array has 2 elements. sizeof(pts) is total size, sizeof(pts[0]) is one element, so 2 elements total.

questions.title: Nested Struct Initialization
questions.explanation: o.in.b is 2, o.c is 3, so 2 + 3 = 5.

questions.title: Offsetof and Alignment
questions.description: What does this program print? Assume char=1, int=4.
questions.explanation: offsetof gives byte offset of a member. char a (1 byte) + padding (3 bytes) puts int b at offset 4.

questions.title: Const Struct Members
questions.explanation: Cannot modify const member after initialization. Assignment to cfg.max_size is a compilation error.

questions.title: Empty Struct Initializer
options: Compilation error in C (OK in C++)
questions.explanation: Empty initializer {} is a C++ feature, not valid in C. In C, use {0} to zero-initialize.
hints: C requires at least one value
